title: Informix Data Packing
text: Informix Data Packing The table shows how Uniface packing codes are mapped to Informix storage formats: Search: Column Uniface Packing Code Informix Storage Format Filter results... 32 rows found Table 1. Mappings Between Uniface Packing Codes and Informix Storage Format Uniface Packing Code Informix Storage Format Filter 32 rows found Filter 32 rows found Uniface Packing Code Informix Storage Format B, B1-B5 Char C1-C* Char D, D1-D11 Date E, E1-E8, E10 Datetime year to second F Decimal F4 Real F8 Float I1-I2 Smallint I3-I4 Integer I8 Float M1-M4 Money N1-N32 Decimal (N)C1-32 Decimal (N)Cn=Numeric data type packed with C packing code. O1-O32 Decimal P1-P16 Decimal Q1-Q16 Decimal R1-R* Char (S)A1-A9 Char (S)An=String data type packed with A packing code. (S)A10 Serial (S)A11-A40 Char SR1-SR* Byte SC1-SC* Text SU1-SU* Text T, T1-T2 Char T3 Datetime year to second U1-U* Char VC1-VC255 Varchar VC256-VC* Char VR1-VR255 Varchar VR256-VR* Char VU1-VU255 Varchar VU256-VU* Char The following table is intended as a guide, not a complete discussion of Informix storage formats. If you are in any doubt about the exact requirements in your environment, see your Informix documentation for more information. Table 2. Informix Storage Formats Storage format Explanation Byte Byte fields contain undifferentiated byte streams. Any raw data is allowed. See the Text storage format for additional information. Char Char fields can contain any ASCII character, have a maximum length of 32511 bytes and are left-justified. Char fields can contain more data than Uniface currently supports in a fixed-length field. Date Date fields store calendar dates in the format ‘mm/dd/yyyy’. The values range from ‘01/01/0001’ through ‘12/31/9999’. A Date data type is stored internally as a four-byte integer which represents the number of days since December 31, 1899. Date fields are all eight-byte fields as far as the INF connector is concerned. However, Informix stores these as four-byte fields. If an index contains one or more date fields, Uniface may calculate the maximum length to be more than the maximum index length. This happens because of the extra four bytes included for each date field in the index. In such a case, Uniface does not create the index, even if it is less than the maximum Informix index length. Datetime Datetime fields store instances of time expressed as a calendar date and time of day according to a particular precision. The chosen precision for Uniface is ‘Datetime year to second’ which is in the format ‘yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss’. The values range from ‘0001-01-01 00:00:00’ through ‘9999-12-31 23:59:59’. A Datetime data type with ‘year to second’ precision is internally stored as an eight-byte decimal. Decimal Decimal fields contain a machine-independent representation of a number of up to 32 significant digits, with or without a decimal point and with exponents in the range -128 through 126. Decimals are defined in Informix with the format precision . scaling , using the scaling that is used in the modeled field, if any. For example, the number 1,000,000.00 has ten places. Eight places are before the decimal point, and two places after the decimal point. This is written as 10.2: there are 10 places, with two places after the decimal point. All numbers with an absolute value less than 1.5*10 (—scaling) have the value zero. The largest absolute value of a variable of a Decimal type that you can store without an error is (10 (precision—scaling) —10 (—scaling) ). A negative exponent gives a value of less than 1. Fields with packing codes N32, O31, O32, P16, or Q16 can drop significant digits if you try to store the maximum number of allowed digits in such fields. This is due to the internal remapping and conversion that takes place before Uniface can map to Decimal. Float Float fields contain eight-byte floating point machine-independent binary numbers. Integer Integer fields contain signed integer values. Possible values are in the range -2147483647 through 2147483647. Interval Interval has no associated Uniface packing code. It can, however, be read and written as a character string. Money Money fields contain decimal numbers with a scaling of two (19.2), or with a scaling of four (19.4), depending on the Uniface packing code used. Nchar Nchar fields can contain any national characters and are similar to their non-NLS counterpart Char fields. Nchar storage formats can only be created, accessed, and manipulated in an NLS environment. Nvarchar Nvarchar fields can contain any national-characters and are basically the same as their non-NLS counterpart Varchar fields. Nvarchar storage formats can only be created, accessed, and manipulated in an NLS environment. Real Real fields, also known as Smallfloat fields, contain four-byte floating point, machine-independent binary numbers. Serial Serial fields provide positive sequential integer values which Informix generates automatically. Each table can have at most one Serial field and is automatically not nullable. A unique constraint must be set to prevent duplicate Serial values since it is not automatically a unique column. A Serial field created through the connector will use the default initial start value 1. However, an initial start value can be specified using the alter table statement. This can be done via SQL Edit, the sql instruction or the Informix front-end product. For example: alter TableName modify ( SerialField serial ( StartValue) ); Another alternative is to run an SQL script generated by the Create Table facility. The default start value 1 can then easily be modified to a specified value in the SQL script file. The connector will, by default, never insert values into Serial fields. Instead, Informix provides the actual value for the Serial field. Therefore, entities which display the Serial fields must refetch the Serial value after each Write so that Uniface will have the correct value for the Serial field. The connector never updates a Serial field. Inserting specific values into a Serial field is possible if the serial insert connector option is enabled. This is useful for bulk load. By inserting specific values, the starting point is automatically modified. To obtain the correct Serial value after an insert or update, add the following to the store trigger for entities which include Serial fields: trigger store store sql "select SerialField from TableName %/ where pk=%%pk_in_form", "inf" SerialField = $result end where pk is the primary key of the table and pk_in_form is the primary key field of the form. As the value for the Serial field before the store does not match the value after the store, the following warning, which can be ignored, will be issued: 2012 - Occurrence in form does not match database occurrence. Inserting specific values into a Serial field is possible if the serial insert connector option is enabled—this is useful for bulk load. By inserting specific values, the starting point is automatically modified. Do not use Serial fields as primary, candidate, or foreign keys in relationships. In addition, do not used them as primary keys for tables which have accompanying overflow tables. Smallint Smallint fields contain signed integer values. Possible values are in the range -32767 through 32767. Text Text fields are like Byte fields, but with a more limited range of possible contents. Text fields can contain printable ASCII, the tab character, the new line character and the new page character. Text fields do not accept all the characters in the Uniface character set, so the SC* packing code offers only partial support. Text and Byte fields, also known as Binary Large OBjects (BLOBs), can contain 2 31 —1 bytes, while the maximum size of a field in Uniface is 2 32 —1 bytes. It is unlikely that an application would reach these limits. You cannot select, insert, or update BLOB fields in SQL Edit. You cannot define indexes on BLOB fields. Varchar Varchar fields can contain any ASCII character. They have a maximum length of 255 bytes and are left-justified. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
